coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent probe based on tryptophan-coated silver nanoclusters for copper (II) ions detection and bioimaging in cells
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shaoqing
Li, Gufeng
Shi, Hongyu
Yang, Min
Tan, Wei
Wang, Hongbin
Yang, Wenrong
coredata.dc:description: Silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs) have been widely used in sensing and bioimaging owing to their excellent photoluminescence properties and biocompatibility. Herein, we fabricated blue fluorescent tryptophan-coated silver nanoclusters (Trp-Ag NCs) by a facile one-step method for Cu2+ detection and multicolour bioimaging. Trp-Ag NCs displayed a strong blue luminescence with a high quantum yield of 35.6% and a long lifetime of 18.48 ns. In the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence was quenched via static quenching and charge transfer. The developed fluorescence probe for Cu2+ detection showed a high selectivity and sensitivity with a low limit detection of 0.029 μM. Meanwhile, due to their excellent biocompatibility, the demonstration of bioimaging in cells was also provided.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silver nanoclusters
Fluorescent probe
Copper (II) ions
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Trp-Ag NCs
Selectivity and interference of Trp-Ag NCs
The sensing mechanism of the Trp-Ag NCs
Cytotoxicity assays and bioimaging in cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (No. 21665027); the YMU-DEAKIN International Associated Laboratory on Functional Materials, Education Department of Yunnan Province [grant number 117-02001001002107] and the Foundation of Education Bureau of Yunnan Province (No. 2020J0327).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of Trp-Ag NCs (A-B). The particle size distribution of Trp-Ag NCs by DLS (C). FT-IR spectra of the Trp and Trp-Ag NCs (D). Full scan XPS survey spectrum (E) and HR-XPS spectrum of Ag 3d of the Trp-Ag NCs (F).

UV–vis spectrum (black line), fluorescence excitation (red line) and emission spectra (blue line) of Trp-Ag NCs (A), insert: images of Trp-Ag NCs solutions under day light (Ⅰ) and UV lamp at 365 nm (Ⅱ). Fluorescence spectra of Trp-Ag NCs under different excitation wavelengths (B). Fluorescence spectra of Trp-Ag NCs towards the influences of different pH (C), different concentrations of NaCl (D), the irradiation time with a 365 nm UV-lamp (E) and storage time (F), inset: fluorescence intensity under different conditions.

Fluorescence spectra of Trp-Ag NCs with different concentrations of Cu2+ (0–200 μM) (A). A linear calibration plot between ΔF and the positive logarithm of the concentration of Cu2+ (0.1–200 μM) (B). Fluorescence intensity of Trp-Ag NCs upon addition of 20 μM Cu2+ at different incubation times (C). Fluorescence response (ΔF) of Trp-Ag NCs in the presence of different ions at the concentration of 20 μM (red bars), Trp-Ag NCs with 20 μM Cu2+ towards different ions (100 μM, blue bars) (D). Fluorescence lifetime of Trp-Ag NCs in the absence and presence of Cu2+ (20 μM) (E).

Evaluation of cytotoxicity of HeLa cells of Trp-Ag NCs with different concentrations (0–250 μg/mL) (A). Confocal fluorescence image of HeLa cells treated with Trp-Ag NCs (100 μg/mL) at the excitation of 405 nm (B, C and D) and 488 nm (E, F and G). The scale bar is 50 µm. From left to right: fluorescence, bright-field and merge channel fluorescence image.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis of Trp-Ag NCs, Cu2+ detection and bioimaging in cells.
The results of recovery studies of Cu2+ determination in real water samples.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent probe based on tryptophan-coated silver nanoclusters for copper (II) ions detection and bioimaging in cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Resource Clean Conversion in Ethnic Regions, Education Department of Yunnan, School of Chemistry and Environment, Yunnan Minzu University, Kunming 650500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Resource Clean Conversion in Ethnic Regions

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Resource Clean Conversion in Ethnic Regions, Education Department of Yunnan, School of Chemistry and Environment, Yunnan Minzu University, Kunming 650500, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Deakin University, Waurn Ponds, Victoria 3216, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Deakin University, Waurn Ponds, Victoria 3216, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs) have been widely used in sensing and bioimaging owing to their excellent photoluminescence properties and biocompatibility. Herein, we fabricated blue fluorescent tryptophan-coated silver nanoclusters (Trp-Ag NCs) by a facile one-step method for Cu2+ detection and multicolour bioimaging. Trp-Ag NCs displayed a strong blue luminescence with a high quantum yield of 35.6% and a long lifetime of 18.48 ns. In the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence was quenched via static quenching and charge transfer. The developed fluorescence probe for Cu2+ detection showed a high selectivity and sensitivity with a low limit detection of 0.029 μM. Meanwhile, due to their excellent biocompatibility, the demonstration of bioimaging in cells was also provided.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper is essential trace nutrient and has tremendous biological functions in animals and plants [1,2]. Nonetheless, owing to the widespread use of Cu2+ in industrial fields, it has caused serious pollution in the environment through the discharge of sewage from factories [3]. On the other hand, the excessive intake of Cu2+ by the human body may lead to a variety of diseases, such as kidney and liver damage and nervous system damage [4]. If we can’t measure it, we can’t manage it. Therefore, it is of urgent need to establish facile and rapid methods for quantitative detection of Cu2+. Currently there are a number of detection methods of Cu2+, such as atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy (AAS or AES) [5,6], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [7], colorimetric detection [8] and electrical/electrochemical detection [9], which have been well developed. Although some of these techniques exhibit good sensitivity and low detection limits, it is still a fundamental challenge to achieve Cu2+ sensing with high sensitivity and selectivity. These techniques lack sensitivity and require large and expensive instruments including complex pre-handling process and professional technical operations, which are unsuitable for rapid detection of Cu2+ in the field [10,11]. Under this circumstance, fluorescence technique has received more attention due to its excellent sensitivity, rapid analysis, simple operation and low cost.

In recent years, some effective strategies have been focused on the development of new fluorescence probes for detection of Cu2+. For example, a new N-heterocyclic fluoran based dye BiF was synthesised for the detection of Cu2+. A six-membered spirolic hydroxyethylhydrazide moiety was designed and selectively triggered by Cu2+ through a specific cascade reaction of Cu2+ that was used to promote spirolactam ring-opening and hydrolysis [12]. In this study, a limit of detection (LOD) of 71.2 nM was reported. Nevertheless, traditional organic dyes require multi-step organic synthesis and complicated purification processes, and have poor water solubility, which are limited in the fields of sensing applications [13]. Furthermore, a N-acetyl-L-cysteine (NALC)-modified CdS quantum dots based on sensor was developed for the detection of Cu2+ with the LOD of 0.48 μM [14]. Unfortunately, semiconductor quantum dots have high toxic and side effects due to heavy metal atoms, which make it possible to potentially impact on human health and the environment [15]. Hence, green and facile synthesis of nano-probes is urgently needed.

Metal nanoclusters (MNCs), as a fluorescence nanomaterial, has shown enormous applications in the field of chemical in sensing [16,17], bioimaging [18,19], therapeutic applications [20] and catalysis [21]. This stems from their superiority in ease of synthesis, unique optical properties, low cytotoxicity, and good biocompatibility [22,23]. Compared with gold and copper nanoclusters, silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs) have the advantages of lower cost than Au NCs. Further, since Cu has a higher oxidation potential than Au and Ag, Cu NCs are more easily oxidized [24,25]. Thus, silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs) have been extensively explored for luminescence-based sensing and bioimaging. The various synthesis techniques of Ag NCs have been developed including the top-down and the bottom-up approaches [26]. Typically, the bottom-up reduction of metal-capped ligand complexes by reducing agents is a very efficient method [27,28]. These ligands capped Ag NCs have been used to detect metal ions [16,29], small biomolecules [28], biomacromolecules [30], and so on. Although significant progress on synthesis and development of Ag NCs fluorescence probes has been achieved in the last decade, some of them still remain certain challenges such as additional reducing agents, long reaction time, irreversible aggregation due to their high surface energy, and low quantum yields [31].

Microwave-assisted heating technology has attracted much attention due to its high reaction rate, low thermal gradient effect and high reproducibility [27]. In this study, we designed a simple one-step method to fabricate blue fluorescent tryptophan-coated silver nanoclusters (Trp-Ag NCs) for Cu2+ determination and multicolour bioimaging. These blue-emitting Ag nanoclusters (λem = 450 nm) with a quantum yield of 35.6% exhibited excellent photoluminescence stability. Trp-Ag NCs displayed highly sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ because of the excellent sensing recognition ability of carboxyl and amino groups on the Trp-Ag NCs surface. Besides, Trp-Ag NCs with low toxicity and good biocompatibility, can be easily employed as a potential bioimaging application (Scheme 1 ).

AgNO3 was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). D-Tryptophan (D-Trp), Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, NaCl and potassium hydrogen phthalate were provided from Adamas Reagent Co, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was supplied by GIBCO BRL (Grand Island, USA). Dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM) was purchased from Biosharp Co., Ltd. (Anhui, China). HeLa cells were obtained from Kunming Medical University and Institute of Medical Biology. Cell Counting Kit-8 reagent (CCK-8) was provided from Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). All other chemicals were AR grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Trp-Ag NCs
ce:section.ce:para: Trp-Ag NCs were successfully fabricated via a one-step microwave-assisted method using AgNO3 and tryptophan (Trp) as raw material without adding any other reagents. In brief, 200 μL AgNO3 stock solution (0.1 M) was mixed with 2 mL Trp stock solution (20 mM). The as-prepared solution was placed inside a commercial microwave oven at 540 W for 30 min. Then, the crude Trp-Ag NCs were dissolved in 10 mL aqueous solutions. After centrifugation (10000 rpm, 10 min) to remove the larger particles, the mixture was transferred into a dialysis bag (MWCO: 500–1000 Da) for 24 h to remove unreacted organic molecules. Finally, Trp-Ag NCs powder were collected by vacuum freeze dryer at −40 °C for 48 h, and stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator for further use.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were utilized to characterize the morphology and the particle size of Trp-Ag NCs on JEM-2100plus (JEOL, Japan) and Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK), respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured with a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Nicolet, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed using a Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha + spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). Fluorescence spectra were determined on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were performed on a UV-2100 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime was recorded by a FLS1000 steady-state/lifetime spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, UK). Cellular images were acquired on a Leica SP5 confocal laser scanning microscopy (Leica, Germany). Cell viability was assessed by a PectraMax190 microplate reader (Molecular, USA).

The quantum yield (QY) of Trp-Ag NCs was determined by a widely established relative method [32]. Quinine sulfate was selected as the reference. The QY of Trp-Ag NCs (in water) was calculated via the Eq.:
ϕ x
=
ϕ s
×
k x
k s
×
η x
η s
2

Where ϕ is QY; x and s refer to Trp-Ag NCs and quinine sulfate (QY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4), respectively; k is the slope of absorbance to the integral area of the fluorescence spectra (λex = 360 nm); η represent the refractive index of the solvent (ηx/ηs is about 1 in aqueous solutions) [33].

The titration assay of Cu2+ was carried out as the following: 1 mL of Trp-Ag NCs stock solution (100 μg/mL), 2 mL phthalate buffer solutions (50 mM, pH = 4) and various concentrations (0.01–20 mM) of Cu2+ stock solution were added into a cuvette. After incubating for 1 min, the fluorescent spectra (λex = 374 nm, the excitation/emission slit width: 5 nm) were obtained.

To assess the selectivity of Trp-Ag NCs, different cations (Cu2+, K+, Na+, Ag+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Al3+ and Co3+) and anions (Cl-, Br-, I-, NO3 –, CO3 2–, SO4 2- and PO4 3-) were separately added into this sensing system at the concentration of 20 μM. In addition, in the interference assay, 20 μM Cu2+ and 100 μM of the above ions were mixed with fluorescence probe system together.

The two real water samples were evaluated with the above method. Tap water was directly obtained. River water was gathered from Laoyu River (Kunming, China) and filtered by using 0.45 μm microporous membrane. Various concentrations of Cu2+ (0, 0.5, 20 and 50 μM) were spiked in water samples and detected by fluorescence spectra.

In 96-well plates, 8 × 103 HeLa cells per well were cultured overnight. 100 μL of Trp-Ag NCs with various concentrations (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 150, 200 and 250 μg/mL) were incubated in DMEM for 24 h at 37 °C. Subsequently, the medium was replaced with 110 μL of mixture (100 μL EMEM with 10% FBS and 10 μL CCK-8 reagent) and incubated for 2 h. After being washed by PBS, the absorbance was measured by the enzyme marker at the wavelength of 450 nm for the calculation of cell viability [34].

The bioimaging with Trp-Ag NCs was measured by using the human cervical cancer cells (HeLa). Firstly, HeLa cells (each well plate containing approximately 2 × 104 cells) were cultured in DMEM for 48 h at 37 ℃ under 5% CO2. After 2 h incubation of 100 µL Trp-Ag NCs (100 µg/mL), the cells were washed with PBS. The fluorescence images were conducted by a confocal laser scanning microscopy at the excitation wavelength of 405 nm and 488 nm, respectively.

The blue-emitting Trp-Ag NCs were synthesized by a microwave-assisted method as described in 2.2. Compared to other synthetic methods, it has obvious advantages such as shorter reaction time and convenient operation [35]. To obtain Trp-Ag NCs with better optical properties, the synthesis conditions were optimized, including microwave power, reaction time and the molar ratio of Trp/AgNO3. Firstly, the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized Trp-Ag NCs gradually increased from 180 W to 540 W and started to decrease after 540 W (Fig. S1A). Based on the above, microwave power of 540 W was chosen in our experiments. Also, the fluorescence spectra of Trp-Ag NCs at different reaction times were recorded. According to Fig. S1B, the reaction time increased from 5 min to 35 min, indicating that 30 min was selected as the optimal reaction time. Finally, the molar ratio of AgNO3 and Trp was also important. At a fixed concentration of AgNO3, a certain amount of Trp was needed to protect Ag NCs. Fig. S1C shows Trp-Ag NCs has much stronger fluorescence with the increase of the concentration of Trp. The fluorescence of Trp-Ag NCs was the strongest when the molar ratio of Trp/AgNO3 was 2:1.

First, the morphology of the synthesized Trp-Ag NCs was characterized by TEM. As described in Fig. 1 A-B, Trp-Ag NCs presents uniformly dispersed with a quasi-spherical shape. Fig. 1C shows the nanoparticle size distribution in the range of 3–6 nm with an average size of 4.3 ± 0.89 nm. This small size of Ag NCs was attributed to the effective binding of the Trp template and the limited space. Functional groups of Trp on the Ag NCs surface were further analyzed by FT-IR shown in Fig. 1D. We observed that the infrared spectra of Trp and Trp-Ag NCs were quite similar. The distinct peaks at 3403 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of –OH and –NH. The peaks at 1593, 1414 and 1099 cm−1 are designated as COO– asymmetric, symmetric stretching modes and C-O stretching vibration, respectively [36]. FT-IR data suggested that free –NH2 and –COOH existed in the synthesized Trp-Ag NCs, which can be coordinated further with metal ions. In addition, compared with the Trp itself, the peaks at 1356 and 743 cm−1 ascribed to the stretching vibration of C-N on the indole ring and –NH plane bending vibration weaken in that of Trp-Ag NCs, indicating that Ag+ may bind to the N atoms of the indole ring [37].

To determine the chemical composition of Trp-Ag NCs, XPS measurements were also carried out. Fig. 1E depicts that four peaks of C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, and Ag 3d in full-scan spectrum of elements of Trp-Ag NCs, which revealed that Ag NCs combined with the Trp. Subsequently, the high-resolution spectrum (HR) XPS of Ag elements was analyzed in Fig. 1F. The XPS spectra of Ag 3d peak of Trp-Ag NCs at 368.3 eV and 374.4 eV, which could ascribe to the binding energies of Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2, respectively. The peaks at 368.4 eV was between Ag (I) (368.8 eV) and Ag (0) (368.4 eV). Additionally, the Ag 3d5/2 peaks at 374.4 eV was divided into Ag (I) of 374.9 eV and Ag (0) of 374.4 eV [24,38]. The above results also demonstrated the successful synthesis of Trp-coated Ag NCs, and the existence of both Ag (I) and Ag (0) played an important role in Trp-Ag NCs.

In order to further confirm formation mechanism of the silver nanoclusters, the Trp and Trp-Ag NCs were analyzed by HR-XPS. For N 1 s spectrum in the Trp can be separated into two peaks at 401.6 eV and 400.2 eV (Fig. S2A), which were attributed to N–H and C-N. However, a new peak at 406.6 eV was observed in the N 1 s spectrum of Trp-Ag NCs (Fig. S2B), which could be attributed to the –NO2. It was suggested the Ag+ was reduced to Ag NCs by the N–H in the Trp and the N–H was oxidized to –NO2 [38], which was consistent with the conclusion obtained by using FT-IR.

To study the optical properties of Trp-Ag NCs, UV–Vis and fluorescence were measured. As presented in Fig. 2 A, an absorption peaks at 365 nm can correspond to the formation of Ag nanoclusters in solution [39]. In the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2B), the fluorescent emission wavelength of Trp-Ag NCs shows almost excitation-independent property with altering the excitation wavelengths from 324 nm to 414 nm. The maximum emission wavelength is located at 450 nm under excitation wavelength at 374 nm. Trp-Ag NCs solution shows a brown (I) under visible light but blue fluorescence (II) under UV lamp (Fig. 2A inset). In addition, Trp-Ag NCs possessed a high quantum yield (QY) of 35.6% in the aqueous solution based on quinine sulfate (QY = 54%, 0.1 M H2SO4) as standard (Fig S3).

For biological sensing applications, the fluorescence stabilities of Trp-Ag NCs, including the effect of pH, ionic strength, irradiation time and storage time were assessed. Fig. 2C shows that an obvious influence of fluorescence intensity of Trp-Ag NCs for pH change from 2.03 to 11.95. In the neutral or alkaline conditions, a distinct lower fluorescence was observed, which was assigned to the abundant carboxyl and amino groups on the Trp-Ag NCs surface. As the pH increases, these groups got deprotonated and destroyed [40], resulting in the quenching fluorescence intensity. Nevertheless, in acid condition, the system displayed more stability owing to the formation of hydrogen bonds [41]. As observed in Fig. 2D, the fluorescence intensity of Trp-Ag NCs had no reduction even under the concentration of 1.0 M NaCl, which indicated an excellent salt stability under high ionic strength conditions. Also, Trp-Ag NCs was performed under different irradiation time (0–60 min) by UV lamp at 365 nm. Trp-Ag NCs still maintain stable even after irradiation for 60 min (Fig. 2E), showing an outgoing resistance to photobleaching. Finally, Fig. 2F displays the fluorescence performance of the Trp-Ag NCs after storage for 30 days, which demonstrated that Trp-Ag NCs could remain stable for a long time. All the above features displayed that the proposed Trp-Ag NCs have excellent optical and colloidal stability.

Based on the good luminescence performance and stability of Trp-Ag NCs, a sensing strategy for the detection of Cu2+ was proposed. Fig. 3 A shows that the fluorescence intensity of Trp-Ag NCs gradually quenched at 450 nm with the increase of Cu2+ concentration (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150 and 200 μM). The fluorescence response (ΔF, ΔF = F0-F, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities at 450 nm in the absence and the presence of Cu2+, respectively) was found to increase regularly. A good linear correlation can be obtained range from 0.1 to 200 μM by the following equation: ΔF = 407.91 + 381.61Lg[Cu2+] (R2 = 0.992) in Fig. 3B. The LOD was evaluated to be 0.029 μM (LOD = 3 N/S, N is the standard deviation of blank solution, S is the slope of the calibration line) [42]. A relative standard deviation (RDS) of 3.45% was obtained for three replicated tests on 20 μM Cu2+, indicating a good reproducibility of the proposed fluorescent probe. To effectively detect Cu2+, the incubation time of Trp-Ag NCs and Cu2+ was investigated (Fig. 3C). The maximum fluorescence quenching was reached in only 1 min and remained stable for the next 30 min, suggesting its fast response for the detection of Cu2+.

Additionally, as shown in Table S1, compared with other fluorescent materials for the detection of Cu2+, the proposed Trp-Ag NCs can achieve a rapid detection of Cu2+ in only 1 min. Moreover, the Trp-Ag NCs as a fluorescence sensor demonstrated a wider linear range and lower LOD than other fluorescent materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference of Trp-Ag NCs
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity and interference are essential elements to build a sensing method. To examine the selectivity of Trp-Ag NCs to Cu2+ in practical applications, different interfering ions were investigated under the same detection conditions, including the cations of K+, Na+, Ag+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Al3+ and Co3+ and the cations of Cl-, Br-, I-, NO3 –, CO3 2–, SO4 2- and PO4 3-. As shown in Fig. 3D, only Cu2+ could significantly increase the fluorescence response, but other ions had little response. Subsequently, the competition experiments were also conducted in the presence of Cu2+ and other ions. We found that ΔF of the Trp-Ag NCs did not change significantly even 5-fold of Cu2+. These results suggested that the detection method based on Trp- Ag NCs fluorescent probe is high selectivity and anti-interference for accurate identification of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing mechanism of the Trp-Ag NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching mechanism have been well studied, including static quenching, dynamic quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), inner filter effect (IFE), dynamic quenching and static quenching [33]. Fig. 3E shows that the average fluorescence lifetime of Trp-Ag NCs and Trp-Ag NCs with 20 μM Cu2+ are 18.84 ns and 17.97 ns in the decay profiles, respectively. Significantly, the fluorescence lifetime of Trp-Ag NCs did not change obviously after adding Cu2+, suggesting that the quenching mechanism was static quenching rather than dynamic quenching.

It was further confirmed whether the fluorescence quenching of Trp-Ag NCs was due to the coordination of Cu2+ with Trp that might impede the charge transfer of ligand-Ag NCs, or the fluorescence quenching caused by the metal–metal interaction [43]. EDTA acts as a strong chelator for Cu2+ competition with Trp-Ag NCs. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of Trp-Ag NCs quenching by 20 μM Cu2+ could be effectively restored (91.2% of the initial value) when 20 μM EDTA was added, which suggested the Cu2+ induced quenching mechanism was charge transfer. In general, the intrinsic paramagnetic properties of Cu2+ and the groups (–COOH and –NH2) of Trp have strong selectivity and binding ability [44], which may form the stable Trp-Cu2+ complexes. This complex can establish an effective charge transfer from Ag NCs to Cu2+, but prevents the charge transfer from the ligand-to-Ag NCs, resulting in fluorescence quenching of Ag NCs [45,46].

To verify the applicability and reliability of Trp-Ag NCs, tap water and lake water were chosen to determine their the Cu2+. Standard Cu2+ solution was added to the pretreated tap water and river water in different concentrations, making the final added concentration of Cu2+ in the mixture at 0, 0.5, 20, and 50 μM before testing, respectively. The analytical results were illustrated in Table 1 , the recoveries obtained for tap water and river water were 96.6–103.8% and 96.8–100.8% with the relative standard deviations (RSD) lower than 5%, respectively. These results further illustrated that this assay has no significant interference in real samples for accurate and reliable detection Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assays and bioimaging in cells
ce:section.ce:para: To test the suitability of the synthesized Trp-Ag NCs for biological applications, the cytotoxicity in vitro was evaluated by CCK-8 assay. The different concentrations (0–250 μg/mL) of Trp-Ag NCs were co-incubated with HeLa cells for 24 h at 37 ℃. In comparison with control group, no significant decrease in cell viability at the Trp-Ag NCs concentration up to 60 µg/mL was observed (Fig. 4 A). The cell viability still maintained at 77.2% even at a high concentration of Trp-Ag NCs (150 µg/mL). This result demonstrated good biocompatibility and low biotoxicity of Trp-Ag NCs.

Then we used a confocal laser scanning microscopic bioimaging in HeLa cells. The strong fluorescence in HeLa cells were observed (Fig. 4B-G) after the cultured HeLa cells were incubated with Trp-Ag NCs for 2 h at 37 ℃. Meanwhile, the cell imaging clearly showed that the cellular uptake level of Trp-Ag NCs was higher in the cytoplasm of HeLa cells, as the cytoplasmic region exhibited strong photoluminescence [47]. Furthermore, blue and green fluorescence were available for the observation under 405 nm and 488 nm wavelength of laser excitation, respectively. This suggests that Trp-Ag NCs could provide a multicolor bioimaging in cells applications.

In short, a facile, eco-friendly and cheap tryptophan-stable silver nanocluster (Trp-Ag NCs) was proposed by a microwave-assisted method with the advantage of water solubility, high stability and excellent optical properties. Since the surface of Trp-Ag NCs has functional groups, such as carboxyl and amino groups, it can provide the coordinate bonds with other ions. On this basis, Trp-Ag NCs could be employed as a fluorescence probe for the Cu2+ determination within a wide range of 0.1–200 μM, with a fast response time (∼1 min) and a low detection limit of 0.029 μM, and gained satisfactory recoveries in real samples. Furthermore, the obtained Trp-Ag NCs have been successfully used for multicolor bioimaging in cells owing to its excellent bio-compatibility and low biotoxicity. Therefore, Trp-Ag NCs can serve as a promising multifunctional fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ sensing and bioimaging.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (No. 21665027); the YMU-DEAKIN International Associated Laboratory on Functional Materials, Education Department of Yunnan Province [grant number 117-02001001002107] and the Foundation of Education Bureau of Yunnan Province (No. 2020J0327).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107222.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ formation of fluorescent copper nanoparticles for ultrafast zero-background Cu2+ detection and its toxicides screening
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Qing, L. Zhu, S. Yang, Z. Cao, X. He, K. Wang, R. Yang, In situ formation of fluorescent copper nanoparticles for ultrafast zero-background Cu2+ detection and its toxicides screening, Biosens. Bioelectron. 78 (2016) 471–476.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent, Freestanding composite films of Au15 for specific metal ion sensing
ACS Appl. Mater. Inter.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. George, E.S. Shibu, S.M. Maliyekkal, M.S. Bootharaju, T. Pradeep, Luminescent, Freestanding composite films of Au15 for specific metal ion sensing, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter. 4 (2012) 639–644.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visual detection of copper(II) ions based on an anionic polythiophene derivative using click chemistry
